topics: 🏺 Putting a jar in רשות הרבים — when it counts as creating a בור
🐂 The bull-mounting case as a structural model for liability
🚿 R' Yose: the ammat hamayim, chrysargyros, and the Roman billet — act before the danger arrives
⚖️ Apportionment when a שור pushes another into a בור (R' Natan, R' Chanania)
💨 Stones moved by wind or kicked into a private domain — borderline בור cases

overview.title: 📖 Overview — When a Jug Becomes a Pit
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The Mishnah's case of המניח את הכד ברשות הרבים opens a chain of questions about when a stationary object becomes a בור for which its owner is liable. Rav, Shmuel and R' Lazar map out the conditions: filling the public domain, sitting at a corner, or being moved at all. The Yerushalmi then anchors this with the bull-mounting baraita — pushing vs. allowing — and uses R' Yose's striking parallels (irrigation flood, Roman tax collector, billeted soldiers) to formulate a principle of timely intervention. The daf closes with the apportionment dispute when an attacking ox knocks another into a pit.
overview.hebrewSource: הַמַּנִּיחַ אֶת הַכַּד בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים... אִם יִטְלֶינָּהּ מִיכָּאן וְיִתְנֶינָּהּ כָּאן הֲרֵי זֶה בוֹר

slides.title: 🏺 Three Views on the Jug in Public Domain
slides.subtitle: When does putting down a vessel make its owner liable as a בור?

boxes.title: 🟦 רב
items: Liable only בִּמְמַלָּא אֶת כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים
If the jug fills the entire public road
Otherwise: it is normal to put things there

boxes.title: 🟪 שמואל
items: Liable if it fills the road or
נְתוּנָה עַל קֶרֶן זָוִית — placed at a corner
Adds the corner case as a hazard

boxes.title: 🟩 רבי לעזר
items: Even if it does not fill the road
Even if not at a corner
אֵין דֶּרֶךְ אָדָם לְהָנִיחָן בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים

callout.label: Trajectory:
callout.text: From narrow (Rav) to broad (R' Lazar). The wider you read, the more often a stationary object counts as a בור.

slides.title: 🪜 The Trap of Picking It Up
slides.subtitle: If your jug is in the road and you move it, have you just dug a pit?

nodes.content:
הָיְתָה מְמַלָּא כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים
Your jug fills the road

אִם יִטְלֶינָּהּ מִיכָּן וְיִתְנֶינָּהּ כָּאן נַעֲשֶׂה בוֹר
Moving it from here to there creates a בור

columns.label: Option A
columns.content:
יִטּוֹל הַמַּקֵּל וִישַׁבְּרֶנָּהּ
Take a stick and break it

columns.label: Option B
columns.content:
יַעֲבוֹר עָלֶיהָ וְאִם נִשְׁבְּרָה נִשְׁבְּרָה
Step over it; if it breaks, it breaks

nodes.content: Better to destroy your own property than to relocate the hazard onto a new spot.

callout.label: Key intuition:
callout.text: An object you abandon in the public domain is not just sitting there — it is a hazard you are still responsible for. Moving it does not solve the problem; it just shifts the בור.

slides.title: 💬 The Bull-Mounting Proof
slides.subtitle: The Yerushalmi imports an animal-damage baraita to settle the jug case.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: נִישְׁמְעִינָהּ מִן הָדָא — let us learn from this baraita.

lines.speaker: B
lines.text: שׁוֹר שֶׁעָלָה עַל חֲבֵירוֹ — a bull mounted another. If the lower owner pulled his out, or it slipped away, and the attacker fell and died → פטור.

lines.speaker: B
lines.text: אִם דְּחָייוֹ וְנָפַל וָמֵת — חַיָיב. If he pushed the attacker, he must pay.

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: Couldn't the attacker's owner say, 'If you had not interfered, I would have paid your damages anyway'?

lines.speaker: A
lines.text: He could answer back: 'If I had not interfered, you would have paid me only חצי נזק (the bull was תם)!' And if it was מועד — 'I'm not going to chase you through the courts.'

callout.label: Why this matters for the jug:
callout.text: Both cases share the same logic — once your property is damaging or being damaged, you have a right to protect yourself, but only by passive removal. Active pushing creates new liability.

slides.title: 🚿 R' Yose's Three Parallels — Timing Is Everything
slides.subtitle: When the threat has not yet arrived, you may divert it. Once it arrives, you may not.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Irrigation flood: רָאָה אַמַּת הַמַּיִם שׁוֹטֶפֶת וּבָאָה לְתוֹךְ שָׂדֵהוּ — before the water reaches your field, you may divert it elsewhere. Once it has reached, you may not.
2️⃣ Chrysargyros (Roman tax): before the collector arrives, you may say 'X is in my profession, Y is in my profession' — directing the bill to others. Once he arrives, forbidden.
3️⃣ Roman billet: before the soldiers arrive in town, you may bribe the quartermaster to send them elsewhere. Once they arrive, forbidden.
🪞 The unifying rule: damage that is still approaching may be redirected. Damage that has landed on someone may not be transferred to another.
🎯 R' Yose reads this principle straight back into the jug case: once your jug is broken, you cannot relocate the hazard onto a new neighbor.

callout.label: Yerushalmi style:
callout.text: Three vivid real-world scenarios — irrigation, taxation, military quartering — woven into a halakhic principle. The Yerushalmi loves to anchor abstractions in lived experience.

slides.title: 📊 Ox Pushes Ox Into a Pit — How to Split the Bill
slides.subtitle: When two damagers act together, the Tannaim disagree on apportionment.
rows: בַּעַל הַשּׁוֹר חַיָיב; pit owner exempt
Ox owner: שני חלקים; pit owner: רביע
Ox owner: שלשה חלקים; pit owner: רביע
Mu'ad pays full → so split full damage
Tam pays half → ox owner only chayav for his half-share, but pit still creates extra exposure

callout.label: The arithmetic puzzle:
callout.text: R' Chanania reads the baraita's numbers as fractions of full damage: ox owner pays 3/4 (since tam pays 1/2 of his portion of two-thirds), pit owner pays 1/4. The point is that even when the immediate cause is the bull, the pit's existence still creates real liability.

summary.principle: A stationary object in the public domain can become a בור the moment it interferes with passage — and once damage is in motion, you may protect your own but you may not push the hazard onto someone else.

flowSteps.content: When does the jug become a בור?
🐂 Bull-mounting proof: passive removal OK; active push = liability
🎓 R' Yose's principle: עַד שֶׁלֹּא נִכְנְסוּ הַמַּיִם — divert before the damage lands; once it lands, the hazard is yours.

quiz.q: According to Rav, when is one liable for putting a jug in רשות הרבים?
o: Always — it is never normal to leave things there
Only when it fills the entire public domain (בִּמְמַלָּא אֶת כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים)
Only at a corner (קֶרֶן זָוִית)
Only if someone trips at night
quiz.e: Rav: liable only if the jug fills the entire road. Shmuel adds the corner case; R' Lazar broadens further.

quiz.q: What does Shmuel add to Rav's position?
o: He requires nightfall as well
He extends liability to the case where the jug is at a corner — נְתוּנָה עַל קֶרֶן זָוִית
He limits liability to weekdays
He exempts the owner if it was a clay jug
quiz.e: Shmuel: אוֹ בִּמְמַלָּא אֶת כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים אוֹ עַד שֶׁתְּהֵא נְתוּנָה עַל קֶרֶן זָוִית.

quiz.q: Why doesn't the daf simply say: pick up the jug and put it down somewhere else?
o: Because moving it counts as transferring on Shabbat
Because אִם יִטְלֶינָּהּ מִיכָּן וְיִתְנֶינָּהּ כָּאן נַעֲשֶׂה בוֹר — relocating just creates a new pit
Because clay vessels cannot be moved once placed
Because only the rabbis are permitted to move public hazards
quiz.e: Picking it up and replacing it elsewhere just creates a fresh בור. The daf suggests breaking it (with a stick or by stepping) instead.

quiz.q: What is R' Yose's principle drawn from the irrigation, chrysargyros, and Roman-billet cases?
o: One may always pay a bribe to avoid Roman taxes
Once damage has reached you, you may not divert it onto someone else; before it reaches you, you may
Water damage and tax damage are halakhically identical
Roman soldiers are exempt from civil liability
quiz.e: Before the flood/collector/soldiers arrive, you may redirect them. Once they have landed on you, you may not push the harm to a neighbor.

quiz.q: According to R' Natan in the case of an ox pushing another ox into a pit (when the attacker is מועד), how is liability split?
o: The ox owner pays everything
The pit owner pays everything
Each pays half (זה נותן מחצה וזה נותן מחצה)
The ox owner pays a quarter and the pit owner three quarters
quiz.e: R' Natan: בְּמוּעָד זֶה נוֹתֵן מֶחֱצָה וְזֶה נוֹתֵן מֶחֱצָה — half and half. The fractions shift in the תם case.
